content.problemStatement:
Kulyash stays in room that has a single bulb and NNN buttons. The bulb is initially **on**.

The initial states of the buttons are stored in a binary string SSS of length NNN — if SiS_iSi​ is 000, the iii-th button is off, and if SiS_iSi​ is 111, the iii-th button is on. If Kulyash toggles any single button then the state of the bulb reverses i.e. the bulb lights up if it was off and vice versa.

Kulyash has toggled some buttons and the final states of the buttons are stored in another binary string RRR of length NNN. He asks you to determine the final state of the bulb.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of buttons.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS — the initial states of the buttons.
The third line of each test case contains a binary string RRR — the final states of the buttons.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the final state of the bulb (000 for off and 111 for on).
constraints: S and R are binary strings of length N
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line integer N, the number of buttons; second line a binary string S of length N (initial button states); third line a binary string R of length N (final button states).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing 1 if the bulb is on after all toggles, otherwise 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: only one button changed (diff=1) → bulb toggles once → off (0). Test case 2: two buttons changed (diff=2) → bulb toggles twice → remains on (1).
The single button changed, diff=1 (odd), so the bulb turns off.
No button changed, diff=0 (even), bulb stays on.

solution.approach: For each test case count the positions i where S[i] != R[i]; this equals the number of button toggles. The bulb starts on, so after an odd number of toggles it becomes off (output 0), after an even number it stays on (output 1). The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        S = next(it)
        R = next(it)
        diff = sum(1 for a, b in zip(S, R) if a != b)
        out.append('1' if diff % 2 == 0 else '0')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
